# Claims — Range 3 (Gamble 1999, lines 5491–8935)

**Source:** Clive Gamble, *The Palaeolithic Societies of Europe* (Cambridge UP, 1999)  
**Claim ID prefix:** R3-  
**Slice:** `range_3_5491_8935.txt`  
**Coverage verified:** Ch.4 “The first European societies 500,000–300,000 years ago” (climate framework, long vs short chronology, raw materials, chaîne opératoire, organic tech, landscapes of habit, locales/gatherings, Bilzingsleben case study, chapter summary); opening of Ch.5 “Neanderthal societies 300,000 to 60,000 years ago” (framework only). OCR table/figure garbage present in places; recoverable numbers extracted carefully.

---

### Claim R3-001
- **Register:** wiki voice (descriptive chronology) / attributed (sources)
- **Target pages:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999; palaeolithic
- **Claim:** Gamble states that a significant palaeomagnetic event at 775±10 Kyr BP marks the switch to normal from reversed magnetic polarity (Matuyama to Brunhes chron) and is recognized as the boundary between the Lower and Middle Pleistocene.
- **Quote:** “a very significant palaeomagnetic event occurs at 775+10 Kyr BP with the switch to normal from reversed magnetic polarity (Bassinot et al. 1994). … This worldwide marker distinguishes the Matuyama from the Brunhes chron and is recognized as the boundary between the Lower and the Middle Pleistocene.”
- **Notes:** Absolute dating via K/Ar on igneous rocks cited (Aitken 1990).

### Claim R3-002
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble 1995c tables in Gamble 1999)
- **Target pages:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999; palaeolithic-rhythms
- **Claim:** As of Gamble’s Table 4.1, Brunhes chron cycles (0.012–0.775 Myr) average ~96.9 Kyr (range 115–75; 8 interglacial/glacial cycles), while Matuyama cycles (0.775–1.636 Myr after Olduvai) average ~40.9 Kyr (range 45–38; 22 cycles)—cycle length more than doubled after 0.775 Myr BP.
- **Quote:** “the length of cycles more than doubled in the Brunhes chron after 0.775 Myr BP as well as becoming more variable.” Table 4.1: Brunhes mean 96.88 Kyr; Matuyama mean 40.91 Kyr.
- **Notes:** Flag OCR issues on adjacent figures; table numbers recoverable.

### Claim R3-003
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble citing consensus stratigraphy)
- **Target pages:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** Gamble reports that the major middle Pleistocene glaciation of northern Europe—Elster (Netherlands/N. Germany), Oka (Russia), Anglian (England)—is widely correlated to OIS 12, dated 427–478 Kyr BP, forming a stratigraphic “hinge.”
- **Quote:** “this major glaciation in the middle Pleistocene is widely correlated to OIS 12, dated between 427-478 Kyr BP (Sibrava 1986:chart 1). … If OIS 12, the Anglian-Elsterian—Oka, forms a hinge in the Pleistocene…”
- **Notes:** Working terrestrial complexes: Cromerian (pre-OIS 12), Holsteinian (OIS 11–9), Saalian (OIS 8–6), last cycle OIS 5e–2.

### Claim R3-004
- **Register:** attributed (van Kolfschoten / Koenigswald & Kolfschoten via Gamble)
- **Target pages:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999; methods related via biostratigraphy; atapuerca-gran-dolina; isernia (new site)
- **Claim:** The Mimomys→Arvicola water-vole transition (loss of molar roots) is used as a middle Pleistocene biostratigraphic clock; on stratigraphic evidence it occurred after 550 Kyr BP and in most of Europe before OIS 13 (Cromer IV).
- **Quote:** “On stratigraphic evidence the Mimomys/Arvicola transition occurred sometime after 550 Kyr BP and in most parts of Europe before OIS 13, the Cromer IV interglacial.”
- **Notes:** At Isernia 20% of molars still show root-formation indications but remain rootless; at Atapuerca ‘Mimomys extinction’ in TD6.

### Claim R3-005
- **Register:** position recording / attributed
- **Target pages:** atapuerca-gran-dolina; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999; modern-human-origins-debate (adjacent colonization chronology)
- **Claim:** Gamble notes that revised palaeomagnetic work (Carbonell et al. 1995; Parés & Pérez-Gonzalez 1995) nearly doubled claimed ages for earliest Atapuerca artifacts and human remains relative to earlier OIS 13 correlations, creating unresolved tension with European vole biostratigraphy (special pleading needed).
- **Quote:** “further analysis of the sediments … has led to a revision of the palaeomagnetic stratigraphy with claims that the earliest artifacts and human remains are almost double the previous ages … One unresolved problem with this new scheme lies in the bio-stratigraphical data where special pleading is needed to accommodate the new dates for Atapuerca within the European sequence of vole evolution.”
- **Notes:** As of Gamble 1999; flag dating dispute.

### Claim R3-006
- **Register:** attributed (Cordy 1992 / Gamble 1995c via Gamble 1999)
- **Target pages:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** After OIS 12, Gamble reports a decline in large carnivore taxa (hyena, large cats including Homotherium and Megantereon, giant short-faced hyena *Pachycrocuta brevirostris*) and a herbivore community increasingly dominated by mammoth/woolly rhino (*Mammuthus/Coelodonta*) of the mammoth steppe.
- **Quote:** “Of particular interest is the decline in the number of large carnivore species, particularly hyena and the large cats which included the sabre- (Homotherium) and dirk- (Megantereon) toothed tigers and the giant short-faced hyena, Pachycrocuta brevirostris … The longer, cooler cycles are now dominated by the mammoth/woolly rhino (Mammuthus/Coelodonta) fauna … aptly described by Guthrie as the mammoth steppe.”
- **Notes:** Table 4.4 species averages; reindeer appears in OIS 12; classic glacial fauna by OIS 8 (e.g. Ariendorf).

### Claim R3-007
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble 1999 synthesizing Guthrie)
- **Target pages:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999; social-landscape-palaeolithic
- **Claim:** Gamble adopts Guthrie’s “plaid” vs “striped” ecological metaphor: mammoth-steppe mosaics (plaid fabric) favoured habitat generalists and higher local diversity, versus Holocene specialist/monocultural “stripes.”
- **Quote:** “These mosaics would contribute to the ecological resilience of the mammoth steppe … using Guthrie’s terms (1984), produced a structure whose ‘fabric’ is compared to the weave in a plaid, rather than a striped, textile … two forms of mosaic — forest and steppe — two plaids with different plant and animal communities.”
- **Notes:** Conceptual apparatus for colonization and resource structure.

### Claim R3-008
- **Register:** wiki voice (descriptive pattern as stated) / attributed
- **Target pages:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999; boxgrove; bilzingsleben; schoningen; hoxne (new); cagny (new)
- **Claim:** For 500–300 Kyr BP, archaeological recovery is predominantly lakeside and riverine (plus some travertine springs and rare caves); most findspots are secondary/derived; flagship primary-context locales include Boxgrove, Miesenheim I, Bilzingsleben, Hoxne, Notarchirico, Cagny.
- **Quote:** “The major environments from which archaeological material dating to the period 500-300 Kyr BP has been recovered … lakeside and riverine settings … Cave occupation is comparatively rare for this period … these are the flagship sites (Gamble 1996b). The vast majority of locales … are in secondary, derived contexts.”
- **Notes:** Cave sequences: Arago, Orgnac III, Grotte Vaufrey XII–XI, Atapuerca.

### Claim R3-009
- **Register:** position recording
- **Target pages:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999; gamble-clive; atapuerca-gran-dolina; homo-heidelbergensis; oldowan-industry; acheulean-industry
- **Claim:** Two views on first European occupation: long chronology (occupation before Brunhes/Matuyama, some claims >1.5 Myr BP; sites in Bonifay & Vandermeersch 1991 table) versus short chronology (earliest undisputed occupation around Cromer IV / OIS 13, ~528–478 Kyr BP). Gamble supports the short chronology.
- **Quote:** “The case for the short chronology, which I also support, rests on the earliest undisputed evidence for human occupation around the Cromer IV interglacial. If this is accepted as OIS 13, which lasted from 528-478 Kyr BP…”
- **Notes:** Long-chronology table sites include Vallonnet, Monte Poggiolo, Prezletice, Korolevo, Atapuerca TD6, etc. Short chronology champions: Farizy; Raynal et al.; Roebroeks & van Kolfschoten.

### Claim R3-010
- **Register:** attributed (Roebroeks & van Kolfschoten 1994 via Gamble Table 4.7)
- **Target pages:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999; acheulean-industry; homo-heidelbergensis
- **Claim:** Regardless of chronology preference, Gamble (after Roebroeks & Kolfschoten) presents a sharp transformation of the European record after ~500 Kyr BP: from contested small “geofact” pebble collections without human remains to large excavated collections, knapping floors with refits, uncontested Acheulean and non-Acheulean industries, and common human remains.
- **Quote:** Table 4.7 contrast “Before 500,000 years ago” vs “After 500,000 years ago”: “Geofacts … Contested ‘primitive’-looking assemblages … No human remains” vs “Large collections … Uncontested Acheulean (biface) and non-Acheulean stone industries … Human remains common.”
- **Notes:** Central empirical hinge of the chapter.

### Claim R3-011
- **Register:** attributed (Roberts et al.; excavators via Gamble)
- **Target pages:** boxgrove; homo-heidelbergensis; stringer-chris; gamble-clive; acheulean-industry
- **Claim:** As of Gamble 1999, Boxgrove (southern England) is assigned to OIS 13 (~500 Kyr BP) on biostratigraphy (e.g. *Stephanorhinus hundsheimensis*, *Ursus deningeri*, *Arvicola terrestris cantiana*); primary-context knapping and butchery on tidal/lagoon silts before a collapsing chalk cliff; bifaces mostly ovate/limande/cordate; tibia attributed to *Homo* cf. *heidelbergensis* (found Dec. 1993) plus two lower teeth from another individual.
- **Quote:** “Occupation took place … during OIS 13 … In December 1993, a robustly built tibia was found which is attributed to Homo cf heidelbergensis (Roberts et al. 1994) and dated with the rest of the archaeology to c. 500 Kyr BP in OIS 13. Two lower teeth from another individual have since been found.”
- **Notes:** No absolute dates for sequence; biface manufacture ~10–15 minutes (Bergman et al. 1990); flint from cliff carried a few hundred metres.

### Claim R3-012
- **Register:** attributed (Thieme via Gamble)
- **Target pages:** schoningen; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999; chaine-operatoire
- **Claim:** Schöningen peat deposits (eastern Germany) preserve fauna and wooden artifacts including ~2.5 m throwing spears and a 78 cm thrusting spear pointed at both ends; site 12 has a probable hafting splice; three throwing spears made from felled spruce with tips from basal hard wood.
- **Quote:** “the peat deposits at Schöningen in eastern Germany preserve both fauna and wooden artifacts, including 2.5-m-long spears (Thieme 1997, Thieme and Maier 1995). … At site 13 a complete short thrusting spear, 78cm in length and pointed at both ends was found. Longer throwing spears, 2.5m in length, have also been excavated … Three of these are made from felled spruce trees … with the tips worked from the hard dense wood at the base of the tree.”
- **Notes:** Also Clacton broken spear point (Oakley et al. 1977); wood pieces at Torralba and Bilzingsleben.

### Claim R3-013
- **Register:** position recording / attributed (A. Turner 1992)
- **Target pages:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999; gamble-clive
- **Claim:** A. Turner (1992) argues that a large carnivore guild (flesh-eaters vs carcass-destroyers) blocked European colonization until ~500 Kyr BP, when decline of giant hyenas and giant cats opened a significant scavenging niche; occupation before then would have been sporadic.
- **Quote:** “at 500 Kyr BP … there was a dramatic decline in both the giant hyenas (Pachycrocuta brevirostris and Pachycrocuta perrieri) and the giant cats (Homotherium latidens and Megantereon cultridens). Turner argues that … the change in carcass destroyers and flesh eaters opened up, for the first time, a significant niche for hominid scavenging.”
- **Notes:** Gamble finds this attractive for sparse pre-500kyr record but insists the post-500kyr change is a “complete transformation” best explained by first occupation.

### Claim R3-014
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble’s own social-colonization argument)
- **Target pages:** gamble-clive; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999; social-landscape-palaeolithic; intimate-effective-extended-networks; stringer-chris
- **Claim:** Gamble argues late European colonization was a *social* constraint: extreme seasonality on plains required greater annual mobility and population fission distances that middle Pleistocene social systems could not support; colonization required continental-scale simultaneous occupation (all but NE region) to sustain demography without constant immigration.
- **Quote:** “this seasonality could only be overcome through greater annual mobility and the fissioning of population. These necessary distances and separation of population … could not be supported by the mechanisms available to middle Pleistocene social systems (Gamble 1993a, 1993d, Stringer and Gamble 1993:figure 82). … They were social beings … not simply dancing to the rhythms of the Pleistocene.”
- **Notes:** Links social systems to resource “plaid” structure and carnivore change.

### Claim R3-015
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble 1999 social-technology program)
- **Target pages:** palaeolithic-rhythms; chaine-operatoire; social-landscape-palaeolithic; locale; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** Gamble frames lithics as social technology preserving orders of temporality; three analysis rhythms: (1) raw-material acquisition/transfer (paths and tracks); (2) technology/tool production (chaîne opératoire); (3) skills creating the surrounding environment (taskscape).
- **Quote:** “Lithics provide the key … They preserve different orders of temporality in their acquisition, manufacture and discard. … we are dealing with a social technology. … The three rhythms involve analysing • raw-material acquisition and transfer … • the technology and tool production covered by the chaine opératoire • the skills involved in creating the environment surrounding the hominid, the taskscape.”
- **Notes:** Cites Perlés 1992 on conceptual/technical/economic simultaneity.

### Claim R3-016
- **Register:** attributed (Lebel 1992 / Féblot-Augustins 1997 via Gamble)
- **Target pages:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999; locale; arago (new site); acheulean-industry
- **Claim:** For this period raw materials are predominantly local: at Arago (levels D–G, OIS 12) ~80% within 5 km (Geneste principle), max 35 km; Europe-wide sample of 94 transfers from 33 sites/layers had only 15 >30 km, max 80 km (Labastide d’Anjou), mean furthest transfer 28 km; no transfers beyond the 80–100 km threshold and no marine molluscs, fossil shells, ochre, or ivory transfers.
- **Quote:** “the majority of lithic materials (80 per cent) came from within 5km of the cave … The furthest distance was 35 km … For all Europe a sample of ninety-four raw material transfers from thirty-three sites/layers produced only fifteen which were greater than 30km. The largest distance was 80km … The transfer of materials beyond the 80-100-km threshold … is not found during this period.”
- **Notes:** Fine-grained flints from 7–30 km show later stages of chaîne only (cortex scarce).

### Claim R3-017
- **Register:** attributed (Ashton et al.; McNabb & Ashton via Gamble)
- **Target pages:** acheulean-industry; boxgrove; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999; chaine-operatoire
- **Claim:** Gamble accepts that the British “Clactonian” is not a distinct cultural entity parallel to the Acheulean: Barnham (OIS 11) and High Lodge (pre-OIS 12 transport) show flake/core and biface outcomes as continuous, situational responses to raw material and micro-habitat, not separate traditions.
- **Quote:** “we can now see that the Clactonian can no longer be regarded as a typological, technological and hence cultural entity distinct from the wider Acheulean tradition. … the decision to follow an Acheulean or a Clactonian reduction sequence was bound up in the immediate context of deploying stone to assist behaviour.”
- **Notes:** Boxgrove GTP 17: six flint blocks to a horse carcass produced irregular flakes (would have been “Clactonian” if only that trench excavated); ovate bifaces elsewhere on same horizon.

### Claim R3-018
- **Register:** attributed (Davidson & Noble; Schlanger; Boéda et al. via Gamble)
- **Target pages:** chaine-operatoire; agency; acheulean-industry; levallois; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** Gamble adopts the “fallacy of the finished artefact” critique and Boéda’s façonnage vs débitage distinction: bifaces as progressive revealing of form in a block vs volume subdivision into blanks; artifact form is social skill negotiated through gestures, not a hard-wired mental template of culture.
- **Quote:** “Davidson and Noble have aptly referred to this approach as the ‘fallacy of the finished artefact’ (1993:365). … façonnage, where the final object, such as a biface … is revealed within the block … rather like a sculptor … and débitage where … the volume of the raw material is sub-divided. … the artifact is not the physical representation of an idea shared by all, but instead a social skill negotiated through gestures…”
- **Notes:** White & Pettitt: façonnage organized to a secant plane (unifying biface and Levallois); débitage as migrating-plane technology.

### Claim R3-019
- **Register:** attributed (White 1995 via Gamble)
- **Target pages:** boxgrove; swanscombe (new); hoxne (new); acheulean-industry; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** White (1995) argues British pointed vs ovate biface groupings track raw-material quality/availability: pointed forms correlate with poorer river-gravel flint; ovates with large primary nodules (e.g. Boxgrove, Slindon); preferred form is ovate maximizing circumferential edge; procurement often immediate rather than best-source.
- **Quote:** “pointed forms correlate with the use of river gravel flint which is generally of poorer flaking quality. … The ovate industries are invariably found in close proximity, as at … Boxgrove, to a source of large sized nodules … White concludes that the preferred biface was an ovate with the implication that maximizing the circumferential edge was the knapper’s goal.”
- **Notes:** Swanscombe OIS 11 sequence: Clactonian lower gravel/loam → pointed Acheulean middle gravels → ovate upper loam as local gravel source buried.

### Claim R3-020
- **Register:** attributed (Steele et al. 1995 experiment via Gamble)
- **Target pages:** chaine-operatoire; acheulean-industry; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** Experimental video analysis (Steele et al. 1995): biface knapping took 301 strikes/24 minutes vs Clactonian core-chopper 78 strikes/6 minutes; preparation time (1–3 s) correlated with motor complexity of next gesture, especially with soft antler hammer—process “in hand,” not aiming at a pre-determined finished shape.
- **Quote:** “The former took 301 strikes and 24 minutes, the latter 78 strikes and 6 minutes. … A very good correlation existed between the length of preparation time (1-3 seconds) and the motor complexity of the following knapping gesture … the process of manufacture by faconnage or by débitage is literally ‘in hand’ (Schlanger 1996:248).”
- **Notes:** Soft hammer used for last 188 biface strikes.

### Claim R3-021
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble on organic tech and curation)
- **Target pages:** schoningen; chaine-operatoire; binford-lewis; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** Gamble notes Schöningen spears appear lost/discarded while still serviceable, paralleling many stone bifaces, and questions whether technology was only carried infrequently and over short distances—counter to Binford’s curation-as-energy-investment notion.
- **Quote:** “The fact that the Schoningen examples seem to have been lost or discarded when still apparently serviceable strikes a chord with the appearance of many of the stone bifaces. Could it be that all items of technology were only carried infrequently and only over short distances? Such behaviour runs counter to the notion that implement curation reflects the degree of energy investment (Binford 1973, 1977).”
- **Notes:** Bone tools/bifaces: Bilzingsleben, Isernia, Fontana Ranuccio, La Polledrara, Castel di Guido elephant-bone biface.

### Claim R3-022
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble’s taskscape theory)
- **Target pages:** social-landscape-palaeolithic; locale; agency; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** Gamble defines the taskscape as continuous related activities/skills surrounding the individual (Gibsonian mutual environment and affordances), not discrete functions; early Palaeolithic skills are characterized as generic/transferable rather than culture-specific specialized solutions (e.g. storage).
- **Quote:** “The taskscape is an array of related activities which carry forward the processes of social life … continuous rather than divided up into discrete functions such as hunting, flint knapping or butchery. … a mutual environment surrounds an individual, moves with that person and provides affordances for action.”
- **Notes:** Contrasts tool-assisted hominids (Binford 1989b) vs sophisticated modern-comparable capabilities (Thieme 1997).

### Claim R3-023
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble comparative interpretation)
- **Target pages:** gamble-clive; social-landscape-palaeolithic; acheulean-industry
- **Claim:** Comparing OIS 11-age Olduvai FLK (Masek Beds, equator) with Swanscombe (~51°N), Gamble finds striking archaeological similarity—low-density living floors, stone and bone, no hearths/postholes/structured camp organization—interpreting this as generic transferable skills, with northern occupation ebbing and flowing rather than evolving specific winter-storage skills.
- **Quote:** “the overwhelming impression from the two locations is one of similarity. … it is the absence at both locales of hearths, post holes … or indeed any structured activity, other than flint knapping or carcass butchery, which is striking … I interpret such similarity … as evidence for generic, or transferable, skills applied to different ecological conditions.”
- **Notes:** FLK: 2,465 lithics/193 tools/~38 m²; Swanscombe lower loams ~375 artifacts/200 m².

### Claim R3-024
- **Register:** attributed (Aiello & Wheeler; Dunbar; Byrne & Whiten via Gamble)
- **Target pages:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999; intimate-effective-extended-networks; schoningen
- **Claim:** Gamble links expensive-tissue (brain vs gut) and social-intellect models to predictions of higher-quality animal foods, larger ranges, and vocal/social grooming supporting larger networks without fully symbolic language; Schöningen spears and doubled raw-material transfer distances (Europe mean furthest 28 km vs Africa 14.6 km) fit carnivore-scale ranging and generic skills (ovates from FLK to Boxgrove).
- **Quote:** “The expensive tissue hypothesis therefore predicts finds such as the Schöningen spears … This is supported by the raw-material transfer data which indicate carnivore- rather than primate-size ranges … with distances of 28km for the furthest transferred stones common. The figure for Lower Palaeolithic Africa is, by comparison, only 14.6km … we have ovate bifaces at FLK … and we have ovates 6,800 km to the north at Boxgrove.”
- **Notes:** Social intellect preferred over tech- or diet-primary brain selection.

### Claim R3-025
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble network model applied to this period)
- **Target pages:** intimate-effective-extended-networks; social-landscape-palaeolithic; gamble-clive; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** Gamble argues 500–300 Kyr BP social skills operated within intimate and effective networks only: temporality short (days or a day); paths local; no social landscape of distanciation via symbolic resources—limiting colonization of highly seasonal northern environments requiring group fragmentation.
- **Quote:** “these social skills were firmly based on the use of emotional and material resources in a hominids’ intimate and effective network … The rhythms were set at most by the activities of a few days. … Consequently there is no evidence for a social landscape which I defined … as the means by which distanciation … was achieved with symbolic resources.”
- **Notes:** Landscapes of habit = multiple individual taskscapes; “locales” preferred over “sites.”

### Claim R3-026
- **Register:** attributed (Wymer/Hosfield/Santonja via Gamble)
- **Target pages:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999; acheulean-industry; locale
- **Claim:** Time-averaged river-terrace “dredger” data show patchy Acheulean concentrations (English Ouse/Thames/Solent after Anglian diversion; Duero catchments) that, after controlling for modern discovery biases (Hosfield), still reflect habitual landscape use where paths crossed productive rivers.
- **Quote:** “with these factors removed we can still see differences in the density and distribution of Acheulean material … The higher density of bifaces and flakes which have been recovered from their gravels therefore reflects the frequency with which the paths in the landscapes of habit crossed them.”
- **Notes:** Flagship locales (Miesenheim I, Bilzingsleben) = exceptional preservation pockets within regional patches.

### Claim R3-027
- **Register:** attributed (excavators via Gamble)
- **Target pages:** notarchirico (new); ethnoarchaeology; binford-lewis; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** At Notarchirico (Venosa), a 24 m² gravel beach exposure yielded an upturned adult elephant skull/mandible with tusks in place, 85 bones and 41 stone artifacts (limestone choppers, pointed bifaces, denticulates, scrapers); excavators argue association reflects human use of head/trunk, not hydraulic jumble, though kill vs scavenge and cut-marks not established.
- **Quote:** “excavations have uncovered in a 24-m² area, on a gravel lake beach, the upturned skull and mandible of an adult elephant (Elephas antiquus). … Altogether eighty-five bones and forty-one stone artifacts were found. … The excavators … conclude that the association is not the result of a hydraulic jumble … but does reflect the human use of the trunk and head … This does not of course establish whether the animal was killed or scavenged.”
- **Notes:** O’Connell et al. Hadza ethnoarchaeology: carcass size explains 75% of butchery-area variation; giraffe multi-area mean diameter 51 m—cautions against assuming Hadza-like social work-party structure at Notarchirico.

### Claim R3-028
- **Register:** attributed (with taphonomic caution)
- **Target pages:** isernia (new); castel-di-guido (new); torralba (new); gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** Italian megafauna–artifact locales (Isernia, La Polledrara, Castel di Guido, Fontana Ranuccio) show complex low-to-high energy fluvial associations, bone flaking, marrow fracturing (bison at Isernia), and bone bifaces (Castel di Guido); Isernia “paved” bone/travertine living floors are hard to sustain given fluvial evidence. Torralba/Karlich Seeufer associations remain ambiguous due to stream sorting.
- **Quote:** “At Aridos the higher energy stream deposits have resulted in the collection of artifacts and smaller bones behind the tusks and skull. … Both here and at Isernia la Pineta and Fontana Ranuccio, there is evidence for flaking bone into artifacts … Such claims [shelters/living floors at Isernia] are difficult to support given the strong evidence for fluvial activity…”
- **Notes:** New sites flagged: isernia, castel-di-guido, torralba, la-polledrara, fontana-ranuccio, aridos, karlich-seeufer.

### Claim R3-029
- **Register:** attributed (de Lumley; Bermúdez; Arsuaga via Gamble)
- **Target pages:** atapuerca-gran-dolina; arago (new); homo-heidelbergensis; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** Cave gatherings: Arago (Tautavel) multilevel faunas track OIS 13–12 prey shifts (deer, horse, mouflon); 44 hominid specimens from ≥15 individuals, mostly sol G (OIS 12), 40% children. Atapuerca TD6 Aurora stratum: 36 hominid fragments with simple stone tools; Sima de los Huesos: ≥32 individuals from >1,000 specimens, max age ~35, few infants; no artifacts; catastrophe vs intentional corpse placement still debated.
- **Quote:** “H. and M-A. de Lumley (1979) report on forty-four hominid specimens coming from at least fifteen individuals mostly from sol G equivalent to OIS 12. Here 40 per cent of the sample came from children… Recent finds in the Aurora stratum in TD6 have produced thirty-six hominid fragments associated with technologically simple stone tools … Sima de los Huesos … remains of at least 32 individuals … Either a single catastrophe killed an entire group, or corpses were intentionally placed in the cave system…”
- **Notes:** As of Gamble 1999 sample sizes; new site arago / tautavel.

### Claim R3-030
- **Register:** attributed (Kretzoi & Dobosi / Vértes via Gamble)
- **Target pages:** bilzingsleben; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999; vertesszolles (new site)
- **Claim:** Vértesszőlős (Hungary travertine) ~350 Kyr BP on Arvicola (Holsteinian-comparable though absolute dates debated): Site I dense fragmented bone + small pebble tools (horse 48%, deer 27%), burnt patches without charcoal, two hominids; Site II carnivore den contrast; Site III animal footprints in wallow. Reminds that not all spring-adjacent deposits are hominid gatherings.
- **Quote:** “Its dating is still debated because the absolute ages for the travertine are considered too young for the fauna. This contains Arvicola terrestris cantiana, suggesting an age of about 350 Kyr BP and therefore comparable to the Holsteinian age for … Bilzingsleben … Sites II and III have no good associated human evidence. But this does not mean that Site I was entirely the outcome of hominid gatherings.”
- **Notes:** New site: vertesszolles.

### Claim R3-031
- **Register:** attributed (Roberts/Pitts; Singer/Wymer; E. Turner via Gamble)
- **Target pages:** boxgrove; hoxne (new); miesenheim (new); locale; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** In-situ foreshore/lakeshore gatherings (Boxgrove GTP 17 horse butchery with six brought flint nodules; Hoxne shoreline pointed bifaces in shallow water, twelve ~1 m stone clusters; Miesenheim I Cromer IV partial skeletons + 100 flints) show low-density scatters without pits/postholes/hearths—Gamble reads “gatherings” not elaborated “social occasions.” Turner finds Miesenheim evidence for scavenging inconclusive (no cut marks/marrow breakage; 8% gnawed).
- **Quote:** “the settlement evidence at this locale points to gatherings rather than social occasions where elaboration by hominids of their immediate environment would be expected. … Turner concludes that Miesenheim, for all its in-situ preservation, was at most a short-stay locale used by hominids working stone. They may also have scavenged the carcasses but the evidence is inconclusive.”
- **Notes:** New sites: hoxne, miesenheim.

### Claim R3-032
- **Register:** attributed (Mania/Schwarcz via Gamble)
- **Target pages:** bilzingsleben; homo-erectus; homo-heidelbergensis; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** Bilzingsleben (Wipper valley, E. Germany): Holsteinian interglacial II horizon sealed by travertine; 230Th-234U and ESR place Bilzingsleben II between ~350–320 and 414–280 Kyr BP (OIS 11 or 9 unresolved); warm oak-mixed forest-steppe with southern exotics; ≥3 hominids (skull fragments/teeth)—Vlček classified as *H. erectus*, others prefer *H. heidelbergensis*; ~600 m² excavated, interpretation focuses on 355 m² shore terrace “campsite.”
- **Quote:** “Absolute dates by Schwarcz (et al. 1988, Mania 1995: 90) using 230Th-234U and ESR place the Bilzingsleben II interglacial between 350-320 Kyr and 414-280 Kyr BP, which confirms its position in the Holsteinian complex, but does not resolve the question of whether it is OIS 11 or OIS 9. … Hominids 1 and 2 were adults and were originally classified by Vlček (1978) as Homo erectus although others believe they should be classified as Homo heidelbergensis…”
- **Notes:** Fan deposits redeposited (incl. Hominid 1); shore sands claimed primary context (Hominid 2).

### Claim R3-033
- **Register:** attributed (Mania fauna via Gamble)
- **Target pages:** bilzingsleben; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** Bilzingsleben large fauna (identified sample): rhino 27%, beaver 14%, red deer 13%, elephant 12%, bear 11%; almost no carnivores/gnawing. Elephant teeth age profile catastrophic, dominated by young (<14 yrs, many 2–6); long bones heavily fragmented for bone-tool raw material. Rhino interpreted by Mania as specialized hunting with selected parts brought in (50% young).
- **Quote:** “Among 256 bones identified to species … the most common are rhino (27 per cent), beaver (14 per cent), red deer (13 per cent), elephant (12 per cent) and bear (11 per cent). … up to 65 per cent of the animals were less than fourteen years old. … By comparison, the rhino remains are interpreted as the result of specialized hunting … Fifty per cent of the remains come from young animals…”
- **Notes:** Gamble notes neither elephant age profile is necessarily hunting (vs prime-age hunting profiles later).

### Claim R3-034
- **Register:** attributed (Mania interpretation) / position (Gamble skeptical)
- **Target pages:** bilzingsleben; locale; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** Mania reconstructs Bilzingsleben with six activity zones including three semi-circular bone/travertine “shelters,” fireplaces, workshop, central paved area (~3–4 m² slabs from ~200 m away) with bison skull and quartzite “anvil,” pebble alignment, diverse local lithics (93% flint <10 km; many <1 cm), bone/wood tools, and “engraved” bones with rhythmic marks Mania regards as symbolic.
- **Quote:** “Three semi-circular structures have been identified in the shore deposits … defined by larger blocks of travertine and animal bones which are thought to have acted as weights to secure a windbreak structure … One of the best-known aspects of Bilzingsleben are the ‘engraved’ bones. … Mania regards as symbolic, geometrical markings.”
- **Notes:** Lithics: Keilmesser, scrapers, Tayac/Quinson points, denticulates, notches (Weber 1986; Mania 1995).

### Claim R3-035
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble’s critique of Mania)
- **Target pages:** bilzingsleben; gamble-clive; social-landscape-palaeolithic; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** Gamble treats Mania’s campsite-as-institutions reconstruction as extreme and unique, not as evidence that early Palaeolithic social life equalled Upper Palaeolithic camps; he refuses both naive social archaeology and the conclusion of “no society,” seeking alternatives that do not require huts/hearths/burials as sole social proof.
- **Quote:** “The reconstruction of Bilzingsleben favoured by Mania represents an extreme case of the evidence for institutions which a social analysis is generally thought to require. … the locale is unique and therefore difficult to assess. … if the paraphernalia of social occasions, our social occasions, such as hearths, huts and burials, is the only means by which we recognize social life, then we either have to discover them … or admit that people at that time had no society…”
- **Notes:** Villa’s Terra Amata refitting (40% of groups cross strata, avg 20 cm vertical displacement) cited as caution against “living floor” shelters.

### Claim R3-036
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble applying Stapert 1992)
- **Target pages:** bilzingsleben; ethnoarchaeology; binford-lewis; locale; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** Stapert ring-and-sector analysis of Bilzingsleben circular “shelter 2” (184 objects) shows unimodal peak at 1.5–2 m—pattern expected of open-air drop zone around a focus, not tent-wall bimodality; Gamble, like Stapert at Rheindahlen, suggests a tree as economical focus (supported by alder/box wood, beaver, macaque), and fallen-tree burning for charcoal-rich third structure.
- **Quote:** “The 184 objects … display a unimodal tendency with the peak occurring between 1.5 and 2m from the notional centre of the shelter … It coincides with the edge of a drop zone around a hearth and does not necessarily require the existence of a structure … Stapert concluded that Rheindahlen was created in the open air and that the empty space was probably a tree … Could this also be the case at Bilzingsleben?”
- **Notes:** Hearths themselves not established for the period—Gamble notes this circularity.

### Claim R3-037
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble Table 4.19 synthesis)
- **Target pages:** boxgrove; bilzingsleben; isernia (new); locale; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** Across Cromer/Holstein open locales, most excavations are under ~300 m²; primary-context sites typically show low lithic totals and low densities; the period’s archaeological signature is low-density lithic deposition. Bilzingsleben is higher but not outliers (e.g. Boxgrove Area A 12.3/m²; Bilzingsleben all areas ~32.1/m²; Isernia high; San Quirce SI extreme 448/m² on only 5.5 m²).
- **Quote:** “The archaeological signature for this period throughout Europe is therefore of low-density use of locales as reflected by the deposition of lithics. Bilzingsleben falls within the higher values in Table 4.19 but is not significantly different from other locales.”
- **Notes:** OCR table density numbers partly garbled; relative pattern is clear. Fluvially mixed sites can produce both low and high densities.

### Claim R3-038
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble speculative social reading—clearly marked)
- **Target pages:** bilzingsleben; locale; agency; intimate-effective-extended-networks; social-landscape-palaeolithic; gamble-clive
- **Claim:** Gamble reinterprets Bilzingsleben as performance at gatherings: anvils carried in and positioned form ~8 m diameter interaction rings; setting an anvil is the “attaching ritual” commencing structured rhythmic activity; co-presence and manufacture performances bind intimate/effective networks but leave no lasting structure once co-presence ends—complex dramas, not corporate institutions or organized social occasions.
- **Quote:** “The important social act, the attaching ritual, was setting up the anvil. … Carrying the anvil a few metres or a few hundred signified the commencement of structured activity … Such co-presence over a distance of 8m … also fostered the use of the space … for performances that involved the rhythms of manufacture … These performances emphasized the transient nature of the gathering and its lack of continuing structure beyond the immediate presence of those at the locale.”
- **Notes:** Explicitly speculative; offered for other locales too (Boxgrove, Miesenheim, Hoxne, Arago, Terra Amata, Vértesszőlős).

### Claim R3-039
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble + Mithen cognitive framing)
- **Target pages:** gamble-clive; social-landscape-palaeolithic; agency; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** Gamble argues early European hominid social life was complex not complicated: encounters dominated by resource affordances; cognitive domains still partitioned (Mithen 1996)—trees/animals as materials/food, not personified symbols for clans or endurance; no symbolic infusion of places; mutual environment not personified.
- **Quote:** “we are dealing with a taskscape of sounds and activity which demand attention, rather than a social landscape that extended co-presence through the use of symbols … Mithen (1996) provides an answer by suggesting that the cognitive domains of these early hominids were still firmly partitioned. … Their mutual environment was not personified.”
- **Notes:** Social life still real; resources invested in intimate/effective networks only.

### Claim R3-040
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble Ch.4 summary)
- **Target pages:** gamble-clive; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999; intimate-effective-extended-networks; social-landscape-palaeolithic; acheulean-industry; palaeolithic-rhythms
- **Claim:** Chapter summary: early Palaeolithic social life was routinized; minimal extension of co-presence beyond the gathering; comparable record across eight occupied European regions; raw material immediate or usually within 30 km; biface symmetry and rare engraved-bone order spring from performance rhythms; bonding relied on affective resources and intense circumscribed effective networks; extended networks barely serviced; colonizing ability constrained by intimate/effective focus and pre-500 Kyr resource/carnivore structure.
- **Quote:** “social life in this period was routinized. Hominids led complex social lives with only minimal extension of co-presence beyond the gathering. … Raw material is immediate or obtained within 30 km and usually less. … Extended networks hardly figured because there simply was not time to service them. … With the accent on the intimate/effective network as the co-operative and social unit there would have been strong selection by environmental forces against range extension.”
- **Notes:** Analytical tack between “dredgers” (50–100 Kyr time-averaged river data) and “flagships” (minutes-scale Boxgrove biface sequences).

### Claim R3-041
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble on Isernia dating)
- **Target pages:** isernia (new); gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** Isernia la Pineta was claimed pre-Brunhes/Matuyama on palaeomagnetism and absolute dates (Coltorti et al. 1982), but *Arvicola terrestris cantiana* indicates a later date during Mimomys–Arvicola transition (OIS 15–13) on biostratigraphic grounds.
- **Quote:** “Palaeomagnetic measurements and absolute dates placed this site below the Brunhes/Matuyama boundary and hence earlier than 775 Kyr BP (Coltorti et al. 1982). However … the presence of Arvicola terrestris cantiana indicates, on bio-stratigraphic grounds, a much later date during the Mimomys to Arvicola transition between OIS 15 and 13.”
- **Notes:** Used to prune long-chronology claims; genuine artifacts retained but redated.

### Claim R3-042
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble on surrounding early sites)
- **Target pages:** homo-erectus; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999; modern-human-origins-debate
- **Claim:** Sites surrounding “Europe” cited in long-chronology discussion include Ubeidiya (Israel, 1–1.5 Myr), Dmanisi (Georgia, 1.6–1.8? Myr, *Homo erectus* mandible), Casablanca (Morocco, 0.9 Myr), Riwat/Pabbi Hills (Pakistan); Dmanisi mandible found beneath *Megantereon* skull. Venta Micena “hominid” reidentified as horse; lithics not accepted as artifacts/manuports.
- **Quote:** “Dmanisi … 1.6-1.8? … Mandible … a robust hominid mandible classified as Homo erectus (Gabunia and Vekua 1995) was found directly beneath a skull of Megantereon … the skull fragments which have been published as hominid specimens from Venta Micena … have been reidentified as a horse skull … The lithic specimens at Venta Micena are not artifacts…”
- **Notes:** Gates-of-Europe wait problem if short chronology accepted.

### Claim R3-043
- **Register:** attributed (Dennell & Roebroeks 1996 via Gamble)
- **Target pages:** atapuerca-gran-dolina; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** Dennell & Roebroeks (1996) modification: older Atapuerca (and possibly Fuente Nueva 3, Orce) dates suggest Mediterranean northern rim may have been occupied earlier than Europe north of Pyrenees and Alps; Casablanca earliest well-stratified tools ~800 Kyr BP comparable to Iberian claims.
- **Quote:** “the older dates for Atapuerca and possibly Fuente Nueva 3 in the Orce basin (Roe 1995) suggest that the northern rim of the Mediterranean may have been occupied at an earlier date than Europe north of the Pyrenees and Alps. The earliest age of 800 Kyr BP for well-stratified stone-tool assemblages at Casablanca is also comparable…”
- **Notes:** Softens pure short chronology for far north vs Mediterranean.

### Claim R3-044
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble on Cagny)
- **Target pages:** cagny (new); gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999; locale
- **Claim:** Cagny la Garenne (Amiens Somme terrace): occupation from OIS 12 into OIS 11; hominids returned to a protected ~12 m-wide area before chalk talus—water, stone, wind shelter marking preferred location (Antoine & Tuffreau 1993).
- **Quote:** “occupation at Cagny la Garenne at Amiens in the 2.7-m terrace of the Somme starts in OIS 12. Hominids were attracted to a small protected area, c. 12m wide, in front of the chalk talus … Water and stone materials, combined with shelter beside the talus from the prevailing wind, singled it out as a preferred location … which was returned to on several occasions.”
- **Notes:** New site: cagny / cagny-la-garenne; also Cagny Cimetiére.

### Claim R3-045
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble Ch.5 opening)
- **Target pages:** neanderthals; homo-heidelbergensis; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999; modern-human-origins-debate; thermoluminescence-dating; stringer-chris
- **Claim:** Chapter 5 opens: three questions about Neanderthal societies (environments coped with; continental social diversity; spoken language). *Homo neanderthalensis* appears 300–250 Kyr BP as regional descendant of *H. heidelbergensis*, Europe and limited Near East only (Stringer & Gamble 1993). Stereotypes of Neanderthals as sole Middle Palaeolithic/Mousterian makers challenged by >90 Kyr BP modern humans at Qafzeh with Mousterian (Valladas et al. 1988 TL) and early northern European blade technologies (~> ages cited) assumed Neanderthal-made in absence of *H. sapiens* fossils older than 35 Kyr BP in Europe.
- **Quote:** “They first appear between 300-250 Kyr BP. … absolute dates of >90 Kyr BP for anatomically modern humans from Qafzeh cave in Israel (Valladas et al. 1988), who were found with Mousterian artifacts. Secondly, similar ages now exist for blade technologies of Upper Palaeolithic form from northern Europe … currently assumed … that they were made by Neanderthals.”
- **Notes:** TL, ESR, U-series revolutionized chronology last 15 years; practical lower limit ~300 Kyr BP; Swanscombe TL dates (202±15.2; 228.8±23.3) conflict with OIS 11 biostratigraphy and are dismissed while noting internal consistency of rapid accumulation.

### Claim R3-046
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble on tool maintenance)
- **Target pages:** boxgrove; chaine-operatoire; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** Little evidence for long-interval tool maintenance: Boxgrove tranchet tip removals and refits indicate local continuous reuse within one activity, not later resharpening after days; emphasis on façonnage over débitage predicts less recycling/modification than later Middle Palaeolithic (Dibble 1987).
- **Quote:** “Tranchet flake removals from the tips of many of the Boxgrove bifaces … point to more than one phase in the use of such implements. But refitting indicates that such reuse was local and presumably part of a continuous activity. … With the emphasis in this period upon façonnage rather than débitage … the recycling, reuse and modification of implements seen in later periods (e.g. Dibble 1987) is not to be expected.”
- **Notes:** Immediate approach also applied to organic artifacts.

### Claim R3-047
- **Register:** attributed (High Lodge / Ashton et al. via Gamble)
- **Target pages:** acheulean-industry; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999; levallois
- **Claim:** High Lodge non-biface industry has end/side scrapers with semi-invasive/semi-scalar/non-stepped retouch once thought Middle Palaeolithic hallmarks; sediments transported as a block in Anglian (OIS 12) ice sheet base, so same age as Boxgrove (~500 Kyr BP)—challenges crude-to-fine chronological schemes (Boxgrove ovates at “beginning”).
- **Quote:** “at High Lodge, the non-biface industry is dominated by flakes which are made into end and side scrapers using semi-invasive, semi-scalar and non-stepped retouch. Such retouch was once thought to be the hallmark of later, Middle Palaeolithic traditions. … They are therefore the same age as the Boxgrove material. … at Boxgrove, dated to 500 Kyr BP, they [ovates] come at the beginning.”
- **Notes:** Typological evolution schemes undermined.

### Claim R3-048
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble on OIS 12 landscape impacts)
- **Target pages:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** OIS 12 ice sheets dramatically remade northern European landscapes: increased loess and periglacial activity in central/eastern Europe; southward diversion of Thames and Rhine; English Channel river system at low sea level.
- **Quote:** “The impact of OIS 12 on the landscapes of northern Europe was particularly dramatic. … The ice sheets of OIS 12 also changed drainage patterns by pushing south the major rivers such as the Thames (Bridgland 1994) and Rhine (Gibbard 1988).”
- **Notes:** Sets background for later river-terrace archaeology (Solent/Thames concentrations).

### Claim R3-049
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble on eoliths / geofacts)
- **Target pages:** gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999; methods-related; oldowan-industry
- **Claim:** Long-chronology artifactual claims must meet Warren’s eolith-debunking standard: nature can produce flakes, bulbs, and edge-knapping; burden of proof on claimants via whole-group survey not selected specimens; East African Olduvai dates encouraged “early-man race” extravagant European claims; many European “pebble tools” are geofacts or poorly dated.
- **Quote:** “The likeness between the flaking produced by Nature and that produced by human agencies is sufficient to shift any burden of proof upon those who maintain the human origin of the stones; and this must not be done by a careful selection of picked specimens, but by a survey of the whole group. (Warren 1920:250) … Absolute early dates for tools and fossil skulls in one part of the world acted in another like setting a speed limit on the motorway.”
- **Notes:** Epistemic method claim for early-site adjudication.

### Claim R3-050
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble on Boxgrove landscape use)
- **Target pages:** boxgrove; locale; acheulean-industry; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** At Boxgrove, flint was tested at the collapsed cliff, carried 200–300 m onto silts, knapped and used; few formal flake tools; most bifaces discarded soon after butchery use on tidal mudflats without further modification; refitting shows primary integrity—“lithic material lies where the users dropped it.”
- **Quote:** “Flint was grubbed from the collapsed cliff, tested for quality, and then carried two or three hundred metres out onto the silts before being knapped and used … most of the bifaces were thrown away without further modification, soon after use as butchering tools on the animal carcasses which were scattered across the exposed tidal mudflats. Refitting the biface reduction scatters emphasizes the integrity of the locale…”
- **Notes:** Exemplar of short-rhythm social technology.

### Claim R3-051
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble on gatherings vs social occasions)
- **Target pages:** locale; social-landscape-palaeolithic; bilzingsleben; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** Across early Palaeolithic locales, evidence is overwhelmingly immediate use of stone, animal, and wood resources; more complex structures (shelters, fireplaces) not yet securely found even in primary contexts; only repeated feature is possible stone/bone consolidation of soft ground (Hoxne, Miesenheim I, Isernia, Bilzingsleben).
- **Quote:** “The evidence is overwhelmingly of the immediate use of resources, be they stone, animal or wood. The only feature that a number of excavators comment on is the possible use of stone and bone to consolidate damp, soft ground (Hoxne, Miesenheim I, Isernia, Bilzingsleben).”
- **Notes:** Underwrites “gatherings” analytical category for this period.

### Claim R3-052
- **Register:** attributed (Gamble Ch.4 on co-operative hunting)
- **Target pages:** intimate-effective-extended-networks; gamble-clive; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999
- **Claim:** Gamble allows that coordinated labour may have killed large animals (as in other social mammals) but finds clearer hunting evidence after 300 Kyr BP; difference of hominid gatherings is that consumption becomes focus of negotiation, intensifying effective networks while extended networks remain weak.
- **Quote:** “Individuals may gather at the carcass of an elephant or beside a drowned herd of bison. They may have killed these animals as the result of co-ordinated labour, although the evidence is clearer on this point after 300 Kyr BP. … The difference with hominids is that at their gatherings the process of consumption becomes the focus for negotiation. As a result effective networks were intense and circumscribed.”
- **Notes:** Bridges to Ch.5 population ebb/flow question.

### Claim R3-053
- **Register:** attributed (Maastricht-Belvédère dating caution)
- **Target pages:** thermoluminescence-dating; gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999; neanderthals
- **Claim:** Even within last 300 Kyr, TL/ESR require care: Maastricht-Belvédère TL 250±20 Kyr BP (OIS 7) and ESR molluscs 220±40, but oceanic molluscan climate fits poorly with low-sea-level OIS 7 continental expectation; Kolfschoten et al. accept OIS 7 as “best fit” without full confidence.
- **Quote:** “The Netherlands site of Maastricht-Belvédére (Roebroeks 1988) has a TL date on burnt flint of 250 + 20 Kyr BP … But these molluscs belong in an oceanic climate and are therefore at odds with the expectation of a more continental climate during a comparatively low sea-level interglacial such as OIS 7 … happy to leave the site in OIS 7 as the current ‘best fit’…”
- **Notes:** Opening methodological frame of Ch.5 (range ends mid-framework).

---

## Extraction notes
- **Actual coverage:** Readable continuous text from ~line 53 of slice (book p.102) through Ch.4 complete and Ch.5 start (~pp.174–175+), truncated mid-dating discussion / OCR map garbage at end of slice.
- **OCR:** Heavy garbage on multipage maps/tables (esp. Table 4.3 sites, Table 4.18 shelter contents, some density cells in 4.19, Ch.5 regional maps). Recoverable numeric claims taken from clean prose and clearer tables only.
- **New sites flagged for main thread (not in allowed list as full slugs):** isernia, hoxne, arago/tautavel, notarchirico, castel-di-guido, swanscombe, cagny(-la-garenne), steinheim (age profile figure only), torralba, miesenheim, vertesszolles, terra-amata, ambrona, clacton, barnham, high-lodge, la-polledrara, fontana-ranuccio, aridos, karlich, maastricht-belvedere, qafzeh (Ch.5).
- **No outside knowledge used.**
- **Claim count:** 53
